detectors.title: Reentrancy vulnerability (ETH transfer before state update)
detectors.description: Function `withdraw` sends ETH via `msg.sender` (line 5) before updating state variable `balances` (line 8). An attacker can re-enter during the ETH transfer to exploit the stale state. Apply checks-effects-interactions (CEI) pattern: update state BEFORE the external call, or use a reentrancy guard.

detectors.title: Unprotected withdrawal function
detectors.description: Function `withdraw` transfers value (ETH or tokens) without access control. Any address can call this function to drain funds. Add `onlyOwner` modifier or `require(msg.sender == owner)` check.

detectors.title: Authentication via tx.origin
detectors.description: Function `transferTo` uses `tx.origin` for authentication (line 2). This is vulnerable to phishing attacks: an attacker can trick the owner into calling a malicious contract, which then calls your contract with the owner's `tx.origin`. Use `msg.sender` instead.
detectors.snippet:
require(tx.origin == owner, "Not owner");
        payable(to).transfer(amount);

detectors.title: Unchecked low-level call return value
detectors.description: Function `ping` has unchecked low-level call (line 2). The return value of `.call` / `.send` is not verified. If the call fails, execution continues silently. Capture and check: `(bool success,) = addr.call{...}(...); require(success);`
detectors.snippet: target.call(abi.encodeWithSignature("pong()"));

detectors.title: Reentrancy vulnerability (ETH transfer before state update)
detectors.description: Function `withdraw` sends ETH via `msg.sender` (line 5) before updating state variable `deposits` (line 8). An attacker can re-enter during the ETH transfer to exploit the stale state. Apply checks-effects-interactions (CEI) pattern: update state BEFORE the external call, or use a reentrancy guard.

detectors.title: Unprotected withdrawal function
detectors.description: Function `withdraw` transfers value (ETH or tokens) without access control. Any address can call this function to drain funds. Add `onlyOwner` modifier or `require(msg.sender == owner)` check.

detectors.title: Unprotected withdrawal function
detectors.description: Function `safeWithdraw` transfers value (ETH or tokens) without access control. Any address can call this function to drain funds. Add `onlyOwner` modifier or `require(msg.sender == owner)` check.

detectors.title: Unprotected withdrawal function
detectors.description: Function `emergencyWithdraw` transfers value (ETH or tokens) without access control. Any address can call this function to drain funds. Add `onlyOwner` modifier or `require(msg.sender == owner)` check.

detectors.title: Authentication via tx.origin
detectors.description: Function `emergencyWithdraw` uses `tx.origin` for authentication (line 2). This is vulnerable to phishing attacks: an attacker can trick the owner into calling a malicious contract, which then calls your contract with the owner's `tx.origin`. Use `msg.sender` instead.

detectors.title: Reentrancy vulnerability (external call before state update)
detectors.description: Function `deposit` makes external call via `token.transferFrom` (line 3) before updating `deposits` (line 4). If the called contract has hooks (ERC777, ERC721 onReceived, etc.), an attacker can re-enter. Update state before external interactions.

detectors.title: Unchecked low-level call return value
detectors.description: Function `deposit` has unchecked low-level call (line 3). The return value of `.call` / `.send` is not verified. If the call fails, execution continues silently. Capture and check: `(bool success,) = addr.call{...}(...); require(success);`

detectors.title: Unchecked ERC20 transfer return value
detectors.description: Function `deposit` calls `token.transferFrom()` (line 3) without checking the return value. Some ERC20 tokens (USDT, BNB) return `false` instead of reverting on failure. The token transfer may silently fail. Use OpenZeppelin's `SafeERC20` library: `using SafeERC20 for IERC20; token.safeTransfer(...)`

detectors.title: Read-only reentrancy (view function reads inconsistent state)
detectors.description: View function `calculateReward` reads state variables `lastDeposit` that are modified by `deposit`, which also makes external calls. During the external call in `deposit`, an attacker can call `calculateReward` and observe inconsistent state (read-only reentrancy). Use a reentrancy guard on view functions that read sensitive state, or ensure state is updated before external calls.

detectors.title: Cross-function reentrancy
detectors.description: Function `deposit` makes external calls without reentrancy protection and shares state variables `lastDeposit` with public function `calculateReward`. An attacker can re-enter via `calculateReward` during the external call and manipulate shared state. Apply nonReentrant modifier to both functions.

detectors.title: Cross-function reentrancy
detectors.description: Function `deposit` makes external calls without reentrancy protection and shares state variables `lastDeposit` with public function `claimReward`. An attacker can re-enter via `claimReward` during the external call and manipulate shared state. Apply nonReentrant modifier to both functions.

detectors.title: State change without event emission
detectors.description: Admin function `setRewardRate` modifies state variables `rewardRate` without emitting an event. Events are essential for off-chain monitoring, UI updates, and detecting suspicious admin actions. Add an event emission.
detectors.snippet:
al onlyOwner {
        rewardRate

detectors.title: Authentication via tx.origin
detectors.description: Function `emergencyTransfer` uses `tx.origin` for authentication (line 2). This is vulnerable to phishing attacks: an attacker can trick the owner into calling a malicious contract, which then calls your contract with the owner's `tx.origin`. Use `msg.sender` instead.

detectors.title: State change without event emission
detectors.description: Admin function `pause` modifies state variables `paused` without emitting an event. Events are essential for off-chain monitoring, UI updates, and detecting suspicious admin actions. Add an event emission.
detectors.snippet:
{
        paused = true;

detectors.title: State change without event emission
detectors.description: Admin function `unpause` modifies state variables `paused` without emitting an event. Events are essential for off-chain monitoring, UI updates, and detecting suspicious admin actions. Add an event emission.
detectors.snippet:
{
        paused = false;

detectors.title: Reentrancy vulnerability (ETH transfer before state update)
detectors.description: Function `claim` sends ETH via `msg.sender` (line 6) before updating state variable `pendingRewards` (line 9). An attacker can re-enter during the ETH transfer to exploit the stale state. Apply checks-effects-interactions (CEI) pattern: update state BEFORE the external call, or use a reentrancy guard.

detectors.title: Unprotected withdrawal function
detectors.description: Function `claim` transfers value (ETH or tokens) without access control. Any address can call this function to drain funds. Add `onlyOwner` modifier or `require(msg.sender == owner)` check.

detectors.title: Cross-function reentrancy
detectors.description: Function `claim` makes external calls without reentrancy protection and shares state variables `pendingRewards` with public function `distributeRewards`. An attacker can re-enter via `distributeRewards` during the external call and manipulate shared state. Apply nonReentrant modifier to both functions.

detectors.title: Unbounded loop over storage array (DoS risk)
detectors.description: Function `distributeRewards` loops over storage array `recipients` without a maximum iteration limit. As the array grows, the function may exceed the block gas limit, making it permanently uncallable (DoS). Add a maximum iteration limit or use pagination.

detectors.title: Hash collision risk with abi.encodePacked
detectors.description: Function `getRandomBonus` uses `abi.encodePacked` with multiple arguments (2 args). encodePacked doesn't pad dynamic types, causing potential hash collisions: `encodePacked("ab","cd") == encodePacked("abc","d")`. Use `abi.encode` instead of `abi.encodePacked`.

detectors.title: Delegatecall to user-controlled address
detectors.description: Function `execute` uses `delegatecall` to user-supplied address `target` (line 3). An attacker can point this to a malicious contract that executes arbitrary code in the context of this contract (including stealing all funds). Validate the target address against a whitelist.

detectors.title: Unchecked low-level call return value
detectors.description: Function `collectFees` has unchecked low-level call (line 3). The return value of `.call` / `.send` is not verified. If the call fails, execution continues silently. Capture and check: `(bool success,) = addr.call{...}(...); require(success);`
